Introduction to SimulationStudy Package

In this document, we illustrate the R package SimulationStudy (https://github.com/tianyi-pan/simulationstudy-package) for simulation study of sampling distributions of trimmed and untrimmed means.

# install.packages('devtools')
# devtools::install_github('tianyi-pan/simulationstudy-package')
library(simulationstudy)

Description of SimulationStudy Package

Trimmed mean is an important robust estimator which is less sensitive to the possible outliers in the sample that might come from contamination rather than the population of interest (Kimber 1983). The trimmed mean for exponential distribution has been investigated when there is contamination in the sample populationWillemain et al. (1992). We focus on the cost of the robust estimator when there is no contamination.

The package simulationstudy was designed to investigate the cost of trimmed mean in terms of efficiency in estimating population mean when there is no contamination.

Following Morris et al. (2019), the simulation study can be summarized in the following ADEMP structure,

  • Aim: investigate the cost of trimmed mean in terms of efficiency in estimating population mean when there is no contamination.
  • Data-generating mechanism: \(X_1, \cdots, X_n \sim \mathrm{EXP}(\lambda)\), where \(\mathrm{EXP}(\lambda)\) represents the exponential distribution with rate parameter \(\lambda\).
  • Estimand: Sampling distribution of trimmed/untrimmed mean.
  • Method: 1) Trimmed mean and 2) Untrimmed mean.
  • Performance measure: 1) Bias and 2) Standard Error (SE)

In this simulationstudy package, we provide functions for running the simulation, and summarizing results in figures and tables. We will introduce the functions in the package, and then discuss the programming checking.

An example for SimulationStudy Package

We will describe how to use the package through an example with the following choice of levels:

  • Rate parameter in Exponential distribution \(\lambda:\) 0.5, 1, 2
  • Sample size \(n\): 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 100, 200, 300
  • Proportion of Trimming: 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5

Main function

The main function of the package is simulation(). The function contains the following arguments,

  • lambda.all: A vector of the rate parameters of the Exponential distribution.
  • n.all: A vector of sample sizes.
  • trim.prop.all: A vector of proportions of trimming.
  • Nsim: Number of simulations.
  • seed: Seed for random number generator.

In this example, we set the following vector for the level of parameters,

## rate parameter in exponential distribution 
lambda.all = c(0.5, 1, 2) 
## sample size
n.all = c(5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 100, 200, 300)
## propotion of trimming 
trim.prop.all = c(0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5)

Then, we pass the arguments into the function simulation(), and set the number of repetition Nsim as 8000.

sim_results <- simulation(lambda.all = lambda.all,
                          n.all = n.all,
                          trim.prop.all = trim.prop.all,
                          Nsim = 8000,
                          seed = 123)
# takes around 10 seconds...

In this function, we also claim the random seed for random number generating to ensure the reproducibility of the simulation study. If the argument Nsim is missing, the simulation function will automatically call the auxiliary function optimal.Nsim() to calculate the optimal choice of the number of repetition, controlling the Monte Carlo standard error (Morris et al. 2019) under a specific level, which defaults as \(1\%\). In the underlying optimal.Nsim() function, the vector of \(\lambda\) and the vector of \(n\) are passed and it will return the the smallest integer to make sure the Monte Carlo standard error under the specific level (set in argument MCSE).

optimal.Nsim(lambda = lambda.all, n = n.all, MCSE = 0.01)
#> [1] 8000

The return of the simulation function is an S3 class, which is named as simtrim. We can check it by the base function class() in R,

class(sim_results)
#> [1] "simtrim"

The following functions for generating tables and figures are all designed as S3 methods for this specific simtrim class.

Example of Constraints and Tests

Function simulation_means:

  • lambda: A postive number
  • n: A positive integer
  • trim.prop: A positive number in [0,0.5]
  • Nsim: A positive integer
  • seed: Non-negative integer or NULL

For testing, we used the unit test framework of R, testthat. You can put your R file for testing in the package’s top-level folder tests/testthat. Here is an example of a testing function:

library(testthat)

# Test 1: Test that simulate_means returns an error with negative lambda
test_that("simulate_means throws error with negative lambda", {
  expect_error(simulate_means(lambda = -1, n = 10, trim.prop = 0.2, Nsim = 100), "lambda must be a positive number")
})
#> Test passed 🥇

After setting up all the test cases, simple run devtools::test() to run all the unit tests.

Summarize Results in Tables

Let’s now summarize simulation results in tables. The S3 method for the simulation results is summary.simtrim. We could simply call the summary() function for the returned simtrim object from simulation() function. The summary function contains the following argument,

  • object: A list of results from simulation function
  • type: A character string specifying the type of table, which can be one of “MSE”, “SE” or “Bias”.
  • lambda.select: A numeric value / vector for lambda if you want to filter results based on specific lambda values. Default is NULL, which indicates all levels are selected
  • trim.prop.select: A numeric value / vector for trim proportion if you want to filter results based on specific trim proportions. Default is NULL, which indicates all levels are selected.
  • n.select: A numeric value / vector for sample size if you want to filter results based on specific sample sizes. Default is NULL, which indicates all levels are selected.

The returned data frame contains four columns besides the values of factors:

  • EST.untrimmed: The estimated value of the untrimmed mean
  • EST.trimmed: The estimated value of the trimmed mean
  • MCSE.untrimmed: The MCSE of the untrimmed mean
  • MCSE.trimmed: The MCSE of the trimmed mean

Bias

We can set the type = "Bias" to generate tables for Bias. We can fix the some factors to check the Bias varying by the other factors. For example, we interested in the cases that \(\lambda = 1\) and propotion of trimming is 0.1,

summary(sim_results, lambda.select = 1, trim.prop.select = 0.1, type = "Bias")
n lambda trim.prop EST.untrimmed EST.trimmed MCSE.untrimmed MCSE.trimmed
5 1 0.1 0.0024 0.0024 0.0050 0.0050
10 1 0.1 -0.0012 -0.1297 0.0035 0.0032
20 1 0.1 -0.0029 -0.1498 0.0025 0.0023
30 1 0.1 -0.0023 -0.1563 0.0020 0.0018
40 1 0.1 -0.0021 -0.1594 0.0017 0.0016
50 1 0.1 -0.0009 -0.1601 0.0016 0.0014
100 1 0.1 -0.0003 -0.1646 0.0011 0.0010
200 1 0.1 -0.0004 -0.1670 0.0008 0.0007
300 1 0.1 -0.0004 -0.1678 0.0006 0.0006

We can also, for example, fix the sample size \(n = 10\), and \(trim.prop = 0.1\) or \(lambda.select = 1\) to check the Bias varying by the other factor,

summary(sim_results, n.select = 10, trim.prop.select = 0.1, type = "Bias")
n lambda trim.prop EST.untrimmed EST.trimmed MCSE.untrimmed MCSE.trimmed
10 0.5 0.1 -0.0024 -0.2595 0.0070 0.0065
10 1.0 0.1 -0.0012 -0.1297 0.0035 0.0032
10 2.0 0.1 -0.0006 -0.0649 0.0017 0.0016 
summary(sim_results, n.select = 10, lambda.select = 1, type = "Bias")
n lambda trim.prop EST.untrimmed EST.trimmed MCSE.untrimmed MCSE.trimmed
10 1 0.0 -0.0012 -0.0012 0.0035 0.0035
10 1 0.1 -0.0012 -0.1297 0.0035 0.0032
10 1 0.2 -0.0012 -0.1961 0.0035 0.0032
10 1 0.5 -0.0012 -0.2548 0.0035 0.0034

Standard Error (SE)

For the tables for standard error, we could simply pass type = SE to the summary function. The other settings are same as those in the Bias tables.

summary(sim_results, n.select = 10, trim.prop.select = 0.1, type = "SE")
n lambda trim.prop Relative.SE EST.untrimmed EST.trimmed MCSE.untrimmed MCSE.trimmed
10 0.5 0.1 1.0783 0.6249 0.5795 0.0049 0.0046
10 1.0 0.1 1.0783 0.3124 0.2897 0.0025 0.0023
10 2.0 0.1 1.0783 0.1562 0.1449 0.0012 0.0011

Mean Square Error (MSE)

Although the MSE is not the main performance measure of this simulation study, we also provide an option for users to report the MSE, simply passing type = MSE. The other settings are same as those in the Bias tables.

summary(sim_results, n.select = 10, lambda.select = 1, type = "MSE")
n lambda trim.prop EST.untrimmed EST.trimmed MCSE.untrimmed MCSE.trimmed
10 1 0.0 0.0976 0.0976 0.0017 0.0017
10 1 0.1 0.0976 0.1008 0.0017 0.0014
10 1 0.2 0.0976 0.1205 0.0017 0.0015
10 1 0.5 0.0976 0.1573 0.0018 0.0018

Summarize Results in Figures

Beside the tables from summary function, we also provide a plot() function to visualize the simulation results in Box plots, and line plots in terms of Bias, SE as well as MSE. We also have an option for QQ-plot to compare the sampling distributions of trimmed and untrimmed means.

The S3 method for the simulation results is plot.simtrim. We could call the plot() function for the returned simtrim object from simulation() function. The plot function contains the following argument,

  • object: A list of results from simulation function
  • type: A character string specifying the type of plot. Can be one of “Box”, “MSE”, “SE”, “Bias”, or “qq”.
  • lambda: A numeric value for lambda if you want to filter results based on a specific lambda value.
  • trim.prop: A numeric value for trim proportion if you want to filter results based on a specific trim proportion.
  • n: A numeric value for sample size if you want to filter results based on a specific sample size.
  • ... : Additional arguments for plotting, such as ‘ul’ and ‘ll’ for upper and lower limits for QQ-plot.

Next, we will show the following figures from the plot function.

Box plot

plot(sim_results, type = "Box", lambda = 1, trim.prop = 0.1)

The levels of factors depends on the research of interests. For example, if we are interested in properties of 0.5 proportion of trimming (indeed median of the sample), we could set trim.prop = 0.5,

plot(sim_results, type = "Box", lambda = 1, trim.prop = 0.5)

QQ-Plot

plot(sim_results, type = "qq", lambda = 1, n = 50)
#> Warning: Removed 3 rows containing missing values (`geom_point()`).

plot(sim_results, type = "qq", lambda = 1, n = 50, ll = 0, ul = 2)

Similarly, the other setting of sample size can be used, such as small sample size n = 5,

plot(sim_results, type = "qq", lambda = 1, n = 5)

Line plot

In the line plot, we set the level of \(\lambda\) and visualize the performance measure varring by sample size and proportion of trimming.

Bias

plot(sim_results, type = "Bias", lambda = 1)

Standard Error (SE)

plot(sim_results, type = "SE", lambda = 1)

Mean Square Error (MSE)

plot(sim_results, type = "MSE", lambda = 1)

The functions described above are the core functions in SimulationStudy package. For more information, you could use ? for the name of function to check the manual page.

References

Chikkagoudar, M., and Kunchur, S. (1980), “Estimation of the mean of an exponential distribution in the presence of an outlier,” Canadian Journal of Statistics, Wiley Online Library, 8, 59–63.
Kimber, A. (1983), “Comparison of some robust estimators of scale in gamma samples with known shape,” Journal of Statistical Computation and simulation, Taylor & Francis, 18, 273–286.
Morris, T. P., White, I. R., and Crowther, M. J. (2019), “Using simulation studies to evaluate statistical methods,” Statistics in medicine, Wiley Online Library, 38, 2074–2102.
Willemain, T. R., Allahverdi, A., Desautels, P., ldredge, J., Gur, O., Panos, G., rinivasan, A., Surtihadi, J., Topal, E., and Miller, M. (1992), “Robust estimation methods for exponential data: A monte-carlo comparison,” Communications in Statistics-Simulation and Computation, Taylor & Francis, 21, 1043–1075.
